currentUse: Active Roman Catholic worship site — the sole church of Holy Family Parish (Diocese of Scranton), 574 Bennett Street, Luzerne, PA 18709; weekend and daily Masses held in the former St. Ann's (Lithuanian) church building
summary: The former Šv. Onos / St. Ann's Lithuanian church on Bennett Street in Luzerne, PA is standing and in active Catholic use as the sole worship site of Holy Family Parish, Diocese of Scranton. St. Ann's, founded August 3, 1924 as one of the diocese's last ethnic parishes, was yoked in 1995 with the German Sacred Heart of Jesus and Slovak St. John Nepomucene parishes as the "Catholic Community of Luzerne" and formally consolidated into Holy Family Parish on December 26, 1999. Of the three churches, Sacred Heart was demolished (its furnishings distributed to poor parishes) and the St. John Nepomucene church and rectory on Miller Street were sold in 2008, leaving the former St. Ann's at 574 Bennett Street as the parish's one worship site — the building survived because it was chosen as the consolidated parish's home. The church built in 1924 as a below-ground "basement church" received its upper worship level in 1960 and later a major renovation of the Bennett Street worship space under Fr. David Cappelloni. Masses run Saturday 5:15 PM, Sunday 9:00 AM, and weekdays 12:10 PM; the pastor is Monsignor David L. Tressler, appointed effective July 1, 2022 while continuing as pastor of Saint Ignatius Loyola Parish in Kingston — a shared-clergy arrangement of the kind the diocese's ongoing Vision 2030 planning process uses ahead of possible further restructuring. Parish life is active: the two linked parishes ran a joint "40 Days of Service" campaign September 15–October 24, 2025, and the parish website carried current announcements as of July 2026. No Lithuanian-language liturgy or heritage programming was found; the Lithuanian identity survives as the parish's documented "roots" history.

developments.headline: St. Ann's yoked with two neighboring ethnic parishes as the Catholic Community of Luzerne
developments.detail: Facing declining membership and a priest shortage, Bishop Timlin assigned one pastor (Fr. Robert Arnold) to St. Ann (Lithuanian), Sacred Heart of Jesus (German), and St. John Nepomucene (Slovak) — three ethnic parishes within a three-block radius in Luzerne.

developments.headline: Consolidation into Holy Family Parish
developments.detail: After parishioner input and voting, the three parishes formally consolidated as Holy Family Parish on the Feast of the Holy Family, December 26, 1999, ending St. Ann's as an independent Lithuanian parish.

developments.headline: Sister churches disposed of; former St. Ann's becomes the sole worship site
developments.detail: Sacred Heart's property was sold and its church demolished, with furnishings distributed to poor parishes nationally. After Sacred Heart School closed in 2007, the St. John Nepomucene rectory and church buildings on Miller Street were sold in 2008, 'leaving one worship site on Bennett Street' — the former St. Ann's — and the pastor's residence moved to Bennett Street. No sale prices or buyers are published on the parish site.

developments.headline: Pastor of Kingston parish takes over Holy Family in shared-clergy arrangement
developments.detail: Monsignor David L. Tressler was appointed Pastor of Holy Family Parish, Luzerne, effective July 1, 2022, while continuing as Pastor of Saint Ignatius Loyola Parish, Kingston — a linked-pastor model consistent with the diocese's Vision 2030 planning drivers (priest decline from 200+ in 2003 to 97 in 2021, with further retirements projected through 2030).

developments.headline: Joint '40 Days of Service' shows active parish life
developments.detail: Between September 15 and October 24, 2025, Holy Family Parish (Luzerne) and Saint Ignatius Loyola Parish (Kingston) ran a joint '40 Days of Service' campaign honoring Msgr. Tressler's 40th anniversary of ordination — food-bank volunteering, school-supply collections, blood drives, and donations to pantries, libraries, and veterans.

sources.title: Parishioners turn gratitude into action during '40 Days of Service' to honor pastor
Holy Family Parish Luzerne (Find A Parish)
Parishioners turn gratitude into action during '40 Days of Service' to honor pastor

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: No contradictions. The archive's core facts are confirmed: the building stands, remains under Roman Catholic (Diocese of Scranton) ownership, and is in active liturgical use — consistent with 'the one Lithuanian church remaining under Catholic ownership' in the northern PA cluster. Refinements: (1) the archive lists founding as 'not stated' — parish history dates founding to August 3, 1924; (2) 'ending: standing' should be refined — the Lithuanian parish as a canonical entity ended via yoking in 1995 and consolidation into Holy Family Parish on December 26, 1999, so the building survives but the parish itself was merged, not continued; (3) its survival was the outcome of a triage in which the German and Slovak sister churches were demolished/sold (2008) and the former St. Ann's was kept as the sole worship site; (4) the parish is now territorial, not Lithuanian — no evidence of Lithuanian-language liturgy or heritage programming was found.
gaps: Sale prices, buyers, and current uses of the disposed Sacred Heart (Charles Street) and St. John Nepomucene (Miller Street) properties are not published on the sources found. The date and scope of the Bennett Street worship-space renovation under Fr. David Cappelloni are unspecified. No press coverage was found of a 2024 centennial of St. Ann's 1924 founding (searched anniversary + Lithuanian terms). No Lithuanian-language Mass, Lithuanian society, or heritage events at Holy Family were found — absence of evidence, not confirmed absence. Vision 2030 diocesan documents reviewed do not name Holy Family Luzerne or St. Ignatius Kingston specifically, so future consolidation exposure is inferred only from the shared-pastor arrangement, not from any announced plan. One caveat on address attribution: the claim that 574 Bennett Street is the former St. Ann building rests on the parish's own history pages (St. Ann's built on Bennett Street; 'one worship site on Bennett Street' remaining after the other two churches were demolished/sold) rather than a sentence stating it outright.
